items.title: Welcome to Non-Deterministic Ramblings
items.text: Welcome to this web site, where I hope to be able to record some slivers of wisdom drawn from many years working in IT. Along the way I have met with people and circumstances, many nice but occasionally some not so pleasant, but from each hopefully I have learned something, and if I record it here I hope that you too may find something of value, if only for any humour.

items.title: "Non-Deterministic"
items.text:
A long, long time ago I studied Computer Science, and while I wasn't very good at the wider mathematics courses, I felt a keen affinity with Discrete Mathemmatics. Part of the discrete maths course concerned itself with Non-Deterministic Finite Automata, or NDFA. By all means follow the links for more information, but in those relatively early days, we studied what is now Automata Theory as a precursor to understanding syntax analysis.

For many years, I joked that for all the time we spent studying NDFA I never had one cross my desk, and I think the probable cause which really defines a lot of my reflections in this blog, are that at the time I studied, we did not have the resources to actually build the range of tools we have now that do use state graphs within them.

More recently I have studied Systems Thinking as a post-graduate abd as part of systems modelling we very much get into Determinism. This is when I think of NDFA and smile to myself, as an NDFA as a state machine could not determine an absolutes state given the input. And so it is with my thinking, in producing this blog my ideas seem to go off in myriad directions, rarely taking teh same path twice from any given starting point.

So please excuse the randomness of the pasts, hopefully they do follow in some sort of logical progression

items.title: "Ramblings"
items.text:
Roughly, I have tried to divide the posts on this into under three broad sections

  • Reflecting - We came, we saw, we experienced, and we would like to help you not to fall into the same traps as maybe we did
  • Thinking - in an area dominated by formal certifications and methods, but latterly a paucity of research finding, we develop heuristics that we think may help explain some of the things we see happening in real-world situations
  • Doing - some words about how I have gone about doing some of the IT things I do

I hope that there is something here that will amuse, maybe promote thoughts of your own, and if it does help in any way then my work here is done.

items.title: Russell
items.text:
Despite attempts using AI to make me look like some sort of secret agent, my career has spanned well over 40 years. Starting at a time when it was called Computing, I have worked in Information Technology aka "IT", battled with the Nexus of Forces, and emerged blinking into the light of Digitalisation. EVen now as I write this during summer 2026, the emergence of AI is changing things once again.

Over that time, I started as a programmer, then moved to Product Management, before spending the last 25 years as variously a Solution Architect, an Enterprise Architect, and latterly focussing on Business Architecture

Academically I have a BSc in Computer Science, an MBA, and an MSC in Systems Thinking in Practice.

Hopefully this site contains aspects drawn from a wide variety of experiences. At the top of this site you'll find links to ways to contact me.

items.title: False Starts
path: How Not to Build a Web Site

items.title: WordPress
items.text: I have been endeavouring to create this web site for several years. When doing my MSc, I did create a WordPress site which exists to this day, but it was something that (a) I kept playing with themes of (never being satisfied with a theme), and (b) I struggled t= that every time I returned to create a post that WordPress had changed the writing tools.
path: How Not to Build a Web Site

items.title: Quarto
items.text:
For various reasons, I ended up using R to do the statistical analysis required for my MSc thesis. THis took me to the Posit tools, notably RStudio.

I was aware of the ability to use RMarkdown as a publishing tool, but cognitively getting my brain around using and publishing made it a step too far at a time when I was trying to learn so many things at the same time

And then Quarto happened... if you haven't seen QUarto from Posit, then it is a well engineered and superbly supported solution, that fits seamlessly into RStudio and so felt very familiar and easy to use (but then RMarkdown was already engineered into RStudio, and so I kink myself that I probably missed a trick there)

So why isn't this site created using Quarto?

Good question! The answer is two fold:

  • Firstly, while the Quarto documentation is excellent as showing how to publish on Posit's own free web hosting, it felt very off the beaten track internet wise.
  • Secondly, Quarto had simply too many options, for me it was back to that cognitive overload, and I seemed to spending way too much time trying to make options that should have worked, work, only to find that under Option A, facility X was not enabled.
  • Thirdly, RStudio takes a bit of getting used to as a serious writing tool. Quarto is very flexible, and the extensive support covers ways to use Quarto withe other IDEs.
  • Finally (this is getting very Monty Python), trying to publish Quarto to GitHub Pages (where you are now) looked quite difficult compared to Posit's won hosting service.

Let me be clear, for serious publishing work, I'd persevere with Quarto as it is just so powerful. Maybe another blog post?
path: How Not to Build a Web Site

items.title: Zensical
items.text:
And so we arrive at this site, created using Zensical. I heard the lead developer, Martin Donath talking about his work on a podcast. Zensical is a major update to a HTML documentation tool.

So yes, I am still climbing my way up a steep learning curve, but I got in the ground floor when, compared to Quarto, Zensical is relatively simple. And more importantly, the publishing to GitHub Pages is pretty well documented, hence my arrivel here.

It hasn't been plain sailing, again maybe another blog post
path: How Not to Build a Web Site

items.title: What Have Learned Along the Way
items.text: That this stuff takes a lot of work
path: How Not to Build a Web Site

items.title: Reflections
items.text: Welcome to the section where I try and suggest learnings from what I hgave encountrered over m any years

items.title: Fads
items.text:
Much grumpiness in this post

This post is about a continual gripe of mine, namely the cycle by which the IT industry goes into some form of ill-thought hype over a seemingly big break through which generates a lot of excitement which abates after about 2 years by which time the damage has been done. Originally posted on my Wordpress site, I have taken the opportunity to update this

items.title: Nothing Changes
items.text:
Did I mention that I have been working in the IT industry for over 40 years? I guess it's something I tend not to mention .... much As I head towards retirement and (hopefully) quieter days, I feel I have earned the right to look at new developments in the world of technology and say, as many before m have: "There is nothing new".

To be fair, this isn't quite true, but I'll come to that at some point. However, for the most part of my career I have seen lots of instances of something new and shiny, maybe the "Best thing since sliced bread", yet oin inspection it has proved to be anything but. The many instances make for good topics to discuss over a suitable libation, but I'll start by looking at the general pattern.

items.title: The pattern of a Fad - or is it a "Hype-Cycle"?
The start
items.text:
It is difficult to find a starting point for any hyped technology, generally the concepts have been around for a long period before someone, somewhere puts it to novel use. It is that novel use that drives the hype for several reasons.

You maybe don't believe me, but try a couple of examples:

  • Big Data - came to wider attention when a Silton Valley start-up combined the performance of in-memory database, with a large data set of test posts harvested from a social media provider to show customer sentiment.

    The technology wasn't new, but larger memory devices made it economic to put data tables in memory. The availability of APIs by social media providers and the availability of high-bandwidth internet connections, made it possible to harvest textual data by hashtags on a scale never imagined

  • Cloud Compute - was made viable by maturing virtual machine software running on processors becoming available with multiple-cores able to process massive workloads, and again that internet bandwidth.

I could keep going bu that would create a litany that doesn't really help the case yet

items.title: Confused Messages
items.text:
I posit that the key to these periods of hype is the initial confusion that abounds, and fosters serious FOMO, firstly on teh part of supply-side who don't want to be seen as behind the curve, and then on the part of consuming organisations who may feel that there is competitive advantage at risk of being lost.

Hindsight, they say, is always 20/20, but for each period of boom, there has been a significant amount of mis-understanding around the real nature of any proposition and what it represents. My personal experience on the part of the big Data example above was being told of the power of understanding customer sentiment ... and that is as true today as it was then. What was either mis-understood, or maybe intentionally left out of teh conversations, was that to get the scale of data needed to do the content analysis required large populations of tech-savvy customers using Twitter to express their views.

items.title: The Same Story
items.text: This ia an adjunct to Confused Messages, and for every technology cycle I have experienced, it is that the same story is repeated by every vendor or advocate. This raises flags for me, if there is only a single example then is there enough experience to justify the claims being made.

items.title: News Articles
items.text:
Yes, I am focused on the Big Data example as it represents the clearest example

Let's continue this theme ... the articles I see in the press tend to also consistently peddle the same story, or limited set of use cases. Generally teh press coverage includes speculation about how the example could be applied elsewhere, and it is this speculation that often gets mis-interpreted as additional use cases. Dif below the covers in these early stages, and generally you'll find yourself circling back to teh one and only real-life example.

items.title: Outcomes
items.text: Over time, some organisations are persuaded to try for themselves. Some can be successful, but others report difficulties and these get picked up and go through a second round of press attention.

items.title: Learnings
items.text:
So what have I taken away from experiences several of these technology fads? 1. If there are very few different examples of how to use it, then how do you know what it can really do? 1. If people blindly repeat the same story, then there isn't a base of experience to mine into 1. Ask questions

Sadly real0-life suggests that people focus on the sizzle, and don't question the current reality. And whilst teh sizzle sounds different ine each case, we do not appear to learn form any of these examples

Fad Sizzle IT Using the technology to deliver 'Information' DOT.COM You can save so much money as people will do it themselves online Enterprise Architecture. Save money by taking a holistic view Big Data See the sentiment of your customers Mobile Applications Customers can do anything at any time Robotic Process Automation Save money by having the computer do the work of people Cloud Computing Save money by letting someone else run your compute AI Save money by displacing people with Agents

So that';s me being grumpy. Maybe I'll write another post highlighting what we did learn

items.title: What is the purpose of an Architect?
items.text:
HWhy can't Architects Agree on what an Architect does?

Get a grouo of architects together and finding consensus on what an architect does, based on our expeirneces arising from our practice, tends to prove very difficult to do. Why si this, and what impact could that have on training
path: What is the purpose of an Architect?
